root_cause: pool-svc had slow query behavior enabled via SLOW_SECONDS=5 with SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED unset. The load generator sent 12 concurrent /order requests while the service DB pool was limited to 5 connections, so every pool connection was held by SELECT pg_sleep(...) calls. Health checks and user order requests then timed out waiting to acquire a database connection even though the service and Postgres were running.
actions_taken: Inspected container status and logs, confirmed pool-svc health failures were connection acquisition timeouts, queried pg_stat_activity and found five active SELECT pg_sleep(...) sessions from pool-svc matching POOL_SIZE=5, checked compose configuration, then changed only pool-svc to set SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1 and recreated only that service with docker compose up -d pool-svc. Verified /healthz and /order returned HTTP 200 under the existing load and confirmed Postgres activity no longer showed sleeping queries.
postmortem: A configuration intended to simulate slow queries allowed order traffic to monopolize the application connection pool, which also served health checks. This created a connection-pool starvation incident rather than a database or host resource outage. Prevention should include separating health checks from the main request pool or reserving pool capacity, adding alerts on pool wait/acquire timeouts and long-held connections, and validating load-related configuration so slow-query/test settings cannot remain enabled in production.
